does: This paper investigates how language models detect out-of-distribution inputs by analyzing existing white-box OOD detection methods like CED, RAUQ, and WildGuard. The authors reveal that these methods suffer from a critical confound: their performance correlates strongly with sequence length rather than genuine OOD signals, and this correlation disappears under length-matched evaluation. They identify the source as attention's logarithmic dependence on input length. To address this, they propose a two-pathway framework decomposing OOD detection into two complementary mechanisms: embedding-based features that capture semantic content (effective for topic shifts) and processing trajectory features that track hidden-state evolution across layers (effective for detecting covert intent with vocabulary overlap). They demonstrate this framework achieves strong performance on vocabulary-distinctive OOD cases and achieves 0.850 AUROC on jailbreak detection, with performance varying along a vocabulary-transparency spectrum.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in white-box interpretability methods for language models and the growing literature on detecting out-of-distribution and adversarial inputs in neural systems. It directly responds to claims made by confidence-scoring approaches in LLMs and connects to broader work on understanding attention mechanisms and their length biases. The paper extends prior work on analyzing what different components of neural networks encode—embeddings versus learned representations—which has roots in interpretability research examining layer-wise representations in deep networks. It also relates to work on adversarial robustness and safety mechanisms in LLMs, particularly jailbreak detection, and builds on the recognition that OOD detection requires understanding both semantic content and processing dynamics rather than relying on single-point statistics.
enables: This framework enables more reliable OOD detection systems by establishing a deconfounding methodology applicable to other proposed detection methods. By identifying the vocabulary-transparency spectrum, it provides guidance for practitioners on which detection approach to use depending on threat model characteristics. The two-pathway decomposition offers a new lens for studying how models process different input types and could support development of more robust safety mechanisms in production LLMs. The finding that trajectory features excel on covert-intent detection opens avenues for improving jailbreak and prompt-injection defenses. Furthermore, the framework could inform architectural design choices and training procedures that enhance genuine OOD sensitivity while reducing spurious length-based signals, and it provides a foundation for future work analyzing other confounds in model-based detection schemes.
